1.synthesis: Peter and John go up to the temple at the hour of prayer, and a man lame from birth, carried daily to the Beautiful Gate to beg, asks them for alms (vv.1–3). Peter fixes his eyes on him: "I have no silver and gold, but what I do have I give to you. In the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, rise up and walk!" (v.6). Taking him by the hand, he raises him, and at once the man's feet and ankles are made strong; he enters the temple "walking and leaping and praising God," and all who see him are filled with wonder (vv.7–10). The first apostolic miracle, done in Jesus' name, shows the risen Lord still healing through his witnesses.
1.historical_context: The temple's "ninth hour" of prayer (v.1, about 3 p.m.) coincided with the afternoon sacrifice, drawing maximum traffic — ideal for a beggar, since almsgiving to the poor was a Torah obligation (Deuteronomy 15:7–11). The Beautiful Gate (v.2), likely the bronze Nicanor Gate between the Court of Women and the Court of Israel, was a prime, highly visible spot. The man's congenital lameness (v.2) was incurable by ancient medicine, underscoring the miracle's completeness. Peter heals "in the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth" (v.6) — the very label Rome had nailed to the cross now invoked as the power of life — and the man's leaping (v.8) enacts Isaiah's promise that "the lame shall leap like a deer" (Isaiah 35:6).
1.literary_structure: This sign launches the first major episode after Pentecost, supplying the occasion for Peter's second sermon (vv.11–26) and the first arrest (Acts 4:1) — a recurring Lukan pattern of miracle, proclamation, and opposition. The threefold physical response — he stands, walks, and enters the temple, then leaps and praises (v.8) — narrates the reversal with exuberant fullness. The man's praise in the temple courts (v.9) gathers the crowd that the sermon will address, and Luke deliberately echoes Jesus' own healings of the lame (cf. Luke 5:17–26), showing the ministry of Jesus continuing through the apostles.
1.theological_application: Peter's words — "I have no silver and gold, but what I do have I give" (v.6) — locate the church's true wealth not in money but in the name and power of Christ. The healing models a ministry that meets people's deepest need rather than only their felt one: the man asked for alms and received his legs. And it shows that the same divine power that worked in Jesus now flows through ordinary believers who act in his name, so that the church's mercy and might alike are gifts received, not possessions owned.
1.christology: The miracle is wrought "in the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth" (v.6): the name carries the full power and presence of the risen Lord, and Peter is plainly the agent, not the source. That the One executed under the placard "of Nazareth" now heals through his name is a pointed reversal — the crucified is alive and active. The leaping man, fulfilling Isaiah's vision of the age of restoration (Isaiah 35:6), is a living sign that in Jesus the promised renewal of creation has begun.

11.synthesis: As the astonished crowd gathers in Solomon's Portico, Peter deflects their gaze from himself: "Why do you stare at us, as though by our own power or piety we had made him walk?" (v.12). The God of the fathers "glorified his servant Jesus," whom they delivered up and denied, "the Author of life," whom God raised from the dead (vv.13–15). By faith in his name this man was made strong (v.16). Peter then calls them to repent so that their sins may be blotted out and "times of refreshing" come (vv.19–20), presenting Jesus as the prophet like Moses (v.22) and the offspring of Abraham in whom all families of the earth are blessed (vv.25–26).
11.historical_context: Solomon's Portico (v.11) was the eastern colonnade of the outer court, a known place for teaching where Jesus himself had taught (John 10:23) and the early church regularly met. Peter's titles for Jesus are steeped in Scripture: "his servant" (pais, v.13) echoes Isaiah's exalted Servant (Isaiah 52:13), and "the Author of life" (archēgos, v.15) names the pioneer who leads the way. The claim that "all the prophets" foretold the Messiah's suffering (v.18) is a sweeping hermeneutical conviction, since first-century Judaism rarely read a suffering Messiah from texts like Isaiah 53. Peter cites the Mosaic prophet of Deuteronomy 18:15 and the Abrahamic promise of Genesis 22:18, the same "offspring" text Paul applies to Christ (Galatians 3:16).
11.literary_structure: The sermon parallels the Pentecost address (Acts 2:14–36) — explaining a Spirit-wrought sign, indicting the hearers for the crucifixion, proclaiming the resurrection, and calling for repentance — but adds a fresh weight of titles: Servant, Holy and Righteous One, Author of life, the Mosaic prophet, Abraham's seed. Its sharpest stroke is the compressed paradox of v.15: "you killed the Author of life, whom God raised." Peter tempers the indictment with mercy — "you acted in ignorance" (v.17) — turning accusation into an invitation to repent, and frames the whole as the fulfillment of "all the prophets" (v.18, v.24).
11.theological_application: True ministry refuses the spotlight: Peter immediately redirects the crowd's amazement away from his own "power or piety" to Christ (v.12), the pattern for all who would serve without stealing God's glory. The call to "repent and turn back, that your sins may be blotted out" (v.19) holds out full forgiveness even to those complicit in the deepest sin — the rejection of Christ. And the promise of "times of refreshing" (v.20) joins personal pardon to the cosmic restoration of all things (v.21), so that repentance opens onto both forgiveness now and renewal at Christ's return.
11.christology: Peter stacks the titles of Jesus higher than anywhere yet: God's glorified "Servant" (v.13), "the Holy and Righteous One" (v.14), "the Author of life" (v.15), the prophet like Moses to whom all must listen (v.22), and the offspring of Abraham through whom the nations are blessed (v.25). Reading the entire prophetic witness as pointing to a suffering and risen Messiah (v.18) is the early church's signature interpretive move, and the Abrahamic blessing of "all the families of the earth" (v.25) already plants the Gentile mission within a sermon to Israel. Jesus is the goal of the law, the prophets, and the covenant alike.
